replies.title: directing the energy of a crowd toward (beneficial) ends
replies.content:
There are ways to do it. The ways I know to do it are Wiccanate. They require considerable practice under the direction of more experienced people. Working with the energy of a crowd is not very hard; it's done all the time by charismatic politicians, rock stars and grifting evangelical ministers. To direct the energy of a crowd to ends that they haven't consented to is manipulative. Directing the energy of a crowd to ends that they agree are beneficial takes both self knowledge and several acquired skills.

I have been a witch and a ritual priestess for decades. Wiccan-style small group ritual typically is structured with a leader conducting a group of willing participants to raise and concentrate magical power which either the leader alone, or the group in unison upon a signal, releases or directs either as an offering to a deity (this is priestly activity) or to effect a spell or other magical intention that the group has agreed upon.

A small group of witches (such as a coven) engaged in a magical ritual has some points in common with a small group of instrumentalists making music, especially if the music is based on a familiar melody played with additional improvisation. if the musicians are skilled and have played together for awhile, they may not need a leader or conductor, but if the group is larger than a quartet, the players are not of equal skill, or the chart is challenging, they may benefit from having a leader. The players need to learn how to balance being absorbed in their own expression of the music and listening to the other players. Knowing the basic music cold helps.

Before you would take on trying to organize a jam with a larger bunch of people who don't play together regularly, some of whom can read music and some who can't, some of whom may show up drunk or stoned to the session, and especially if you are performing in public, you need to practice with a small group where unspoken communication is possible, mistakes are forgiven and you have time for a second and third take if the first one was bad. Otherwise the guy with the loudest instrument or the biggest ego will take over.

The analogy only goes so far because doing magic that is intended to have effects on other people or on events entails some safety awareness that playing music usually does not require.

My main witchcraft tradition regularly used to lead public seasonal rituals that upwards of a hundred people attended. The intent of most of those sabbats was to aid people in experiencing connections with beneficent deities, with the human community, with the natural world, and with the better parts of their own natures. As well as being priestly work, that is a form of magic. Rarely was the energy directed toward mundane external goals, even very general and uncontroversial goals. Consent from a large and heterogeneous group cannot be assumed except in dire emergencies when it's obvious what is needed..

Since these large pubic rituals were intended to affect people's well being and often did so, initiates of the tradition were not allowed to lead public ritual under its auspices until they had considerable experience in doing private small group ritual, had done minor roles in the public sabbats, and demonstrated competence in some of the requisite skills.

replies.title: Re: directing the energy of a crowd toward (beneficial) ends
replies.content: Interesting, so do you know what those skills are that allow one to direct the energy of a crowd (besides charisma) assuming one has their consent? I already have a rather large band (7-10 players usually) and we're  quite tight, I want to learn how to make the best use of the platform that I have for the benefit of all the people present as well as especially the healing of Mama Gaia. replies.title: Re: directing the energy of a crowd toward (beneficial) ends
replies.content:
The first advice I can think of is to tune up your ability to sense the psychic energy of audiences that are paying attention to a performer. Attend and observe other people's shows, especially bands that play music similar to yours. Try to open yourself to sensing the moods of the crowd and how the moods and the connection between the audience and the performers shift over the course of a set. You probably need some empathy to feel what the crowd is feeling, but don't get carried away yourself. Maintain some distance as an observer.

Once you start to get a handle on that (which may happen right away or take repeated efforts, depending on your psychic talents), then start observing what the performers are doing that the audience is responding to. Get a feel for how aware the group leader is of what he or she is doing: is it intentional, intuitive, or completely unconscious and accidental? If you can see auras, observe the leader's aura from time to time during the performance.

You don't want to be firing blind. You want to know what effects your actions and intentions have. If you are working with energy, the obvious sensory cues like applause and silence during the quieter parts are only the beginning.

Equally important, you must learn to preserve your sanity and physical health. You are not going to be conducting the audience like an orchestra by standing in front and waving a baton. You are involved in a conversation with them.

In order to direct their energy, you probably are going to have to take some of it inside. If that is the method you follow, you must learn how to be a channel for stronger energies that come from beyond yourself, without either being burnt up by the force passing through you, or losing your ego awareness completely and becoming a trance medium who doesn't know what he is saying and doing or remember it afterward. You also have to have reliable ways of letting go afterward that don't involve getting blotto drunk. The kinds of regular practices JMG teaches, such as the Middle Pillar, are essential. You can't work safely with outside energies without learning how to make separations and how to banish.

If you get anywhere with this, the next danger is ego inflation.

comments.title: Two Alchemy Questions
comments.content:
Fortuitous choice of picture, as I have two alchemy questions:

Is there an earliest time one can cast the divination to determine what tinctures will be needed for the Celtic Golden Dawn's bardic initiation?

Have you (or any other alchemists in the commentariat) come across a reasonable means for an apartment dweller to reduce the amount of smoke produced when burning herbs during the extraction of salt for a basic tincture? comments.title: cleaning house
comments.content:
A friend I hadn't seen in years was coming back into town and needed a place to stay with his family while they looked for a rental and I offered to let them come stay with us. He used to be a little compulsive and violent but he told me he was only like that with people who wronged him so I figured I was safe. Now reading the naivete of that previous statement makes me shudder. Whatever redeeming qualities were there before are gone and he's clearly on the psychopath scale somewhere between a shady corporate lawyer and Dahmer.

Long story short I did manage to get them to finally leave by being polite but direct and avoiding his provocations so that he as no cause to carry out the type of secret, potentially lethal, and often delayed vengeance that he finds so gratifying. Of course he left a pile of stuff and intends to show up randomly to pick it up "sometime this week" to drag things out as long as possible. After they left we immediately placed the pile outside in the garage, washed the floors, rearranged the furniture and burned a ton of sage/palo santo to get rid of the poopy children smell.

I'm also doing the regular banishing rituals, but want to know if there's a good whole house banishing that works. He doesn't believe in the occult but definitely has malevolent thoughts toward me most likely from jealousy, which I'm guessing were not aroused before because I used to be more of an angry lonely loser with no money. Back then I also didn't notice the narcissism or web of lies either, but maybe he was better at hiding it or something snapped during the intervening years when his circumstances changed. I'm just stunned how far I let it go before realizing what was going on, and the sense of relief my wife and I initially felt was immediately followed by migraines and being totally drained. It's been two days and we're still tired and short tempered.

Thanks in advance for your insight and advice.

replies.title: Re: cleaning house
replies.content: On the next visit this person makes and does not get the stuff out, say you will pack everything and send it to a storage facility, with three months paid. If not picked, the items will be destroyed. Do not let yourself, under any circumstances, to be convinced to not do so. Or offer to send everything to the new address where he lives--if you do this, use the option of required signature upon delivery.

replies.title: Re: cleaning house
replies.content: Thanks that's a good idea and hopefully it won't come to that. My guess is that he's biding his time in hopes that I'll let my defenses down after a few days and he can turn on the charm. I'll definitely make sure he leaves with everything this time.

replies.title: Re: cleaning house
replies.content:
Get a new mop and mop pail, and fill it with hot water. Put in a good solid splash of lemon scented sudsing ammonia, a quarter cup of salt, and three shakes of the hottest hot pepper you can find. (A really hot pepper sauce can be used here in place of powdered pepper.) Wash the floor again, beginning with the kitchen and ending at the front door, always washing each room toward the front door. Pour out the water outside in front of your house, if at all possible in the gutter of the street your house is on. This is a standard hoodoo floor wash and will clear out a noxious condition very well. Then do a banishing ritual in every room of your house, and burn a good purifying and blessing incense in every room -- frankincense works well for this.

You and your wife might also want to do hoodoo cleansing baths to take the noxious energies off yourselves -- do you need instructions? I've repeated the recipe often enough here that it occurs to me that you might know it.

replies.title: Re: cleaning house
replies.content: Is the standard hoodoo floorwash and bath that you often prescribe a bit like natural magic, in that it should work fairly universally, regardless of where you are in the world, and isn't likely to run into conflict with any beneficent magical/spiritual forces that have a relationship with the house or location?

replies.title: Re: cleaning house
replies.content: Those aren't "a bit like" natural magic, they are natural magic. Hoodoo is mostly natural magic, though not entirely, and the hoodoo material I discuss here is from the natural magic end of the tradition. So, yes, they work wherever you are, and don't interfere with beneficent forces.

replies.title: Re: cleaning house
replies.content:
I looked it up on the previous week's post so don't bother. It's a waste of your time and I respect you too much to make you repeat yourself. Whenever I hear you on a podcast where the host hasn't done his homework I feel like it's like using a 18 wheeler as a grocery getter. You must drawing power from the Infinite Patience dimension or something.

Surprisingly enough my wife is on board this time. It's day 3 and our lovely home that we were so excited about still feels kind of "bleh". This might be the gateway drug to get her to stop rolling her eyes at me when I talk about this stuff. Thank you.

replies.title: Re: cleaning house
replies.content:
Then I'd encourage you both to do the hoodoo protection bath. Fun fact: each of you can wash the other's back; pay close attention to the spine and the areas just to either side of it, and always move the cloth from above down. That'll help make sure some of the most vulnerable points of your energy body are thoroughly cleansed; it's also a pleasant thing that spouses can do for each other.

Ask her how she feels after she's done the bath, as compared to how she felt before. She's likely to discover that TSW...

replies.title: Re: cleaning house
replies.content: What would you do if there were carpeted floors involved?

replies.title: Re: cleaning house
replies.content: Take a mixture of kosher salt, cayenne pepper, and the grated skin of a lemon. (You'll need a bunch of it.) Sprinkle it lightly all over the carpeted area. Leave it for an hour, and then vacuum it all up. Throw out the vacuum bag, if it's a bag vacuum; if not, empty the contents of the vacuum into a bag you can seal tightly, take it outside to the trash can at once, and wash it thoroughly with cold water.

replies.title: Re: cleaning house
replies.content: Thank you.

comments.title: Sacred Geometry and Magic
comments.content:
Hello!

In your understanding of magic,

(1) what is the role of sacred geometry in magic?

(2) Is it some sort of vector or recipient for certain powers?

(3) How to incorporate SG in meditative practices?

Thank You!

replies.title: Re: Sacred Geometry and Magic
replies.content:
1) Depends on the system of magic. Some traditions of magic make much use of it, others don't.

2) It can be, depending on how it's used.

3) Get a good introductory book or two on sacred geometry. (One I particularly recommend is Robert Lawlor's Sacred Geometry: Philosophy and Practice.) Using a pen, straightedge and compass -- the standard geometer's tools -- construct each of the geometric patterns in the book, one at a time, following the instructions. Meditate on each pattern, exploring the method of construction and the symbolic and philosophical meaning given in the book; give this as many sessions as you need to in order to learn as much as you can; finish with one pattern before you go onto the next one. When you finish with that book, find another and keep on going.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Is there ever a justification for doing offensive political magic? From what you've said about it, it seems like you should only ever beef up your own side, not attack the opposition. Maybe the only exception would be similar to how self-defence law works - it's allowed with immediate threat (US law) or imminent threat (UK law) of harm being done if action isn't taken right now. But as Dion Fortune didn't do that even in the direst days of WW2, are there situations where that is the right course of action?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Nope. Remember that effective magic requires total unity of focus. You can do magic to build your side up, or you can do magic to tear the other side down; you can't do both. That's why people who do hostile magic so consistently end up with lives that are smoking craters; the energies they could be using to improve their lives and those of the people and causes they support all went into hurting someone else.

More broadly, offensive magic is always a path of weakness and failure. If you have a constructive goal, and at least a little magical knowledge, and if you throw all your efforts into achieving that goal, your chances of achieving it are good. That's why Dion Fortune took the approach she did, and you'll notice that she won.

If you throw any significant amount of energy into trying to harm other people and causes that you think are getting in the way of your goal, that's so much energy that's not going to your goal. At the far end of that path are stupidities of the sort being practiced on the far leftward end of the magical scene these days, where people are so busy binding and hexing and cursing that they've basically forgotten about the things they're supposedly trying to help.

comments.title: Inverted tarot cards
comments.content: What's your interpretation of inverted tarot cards? Some people say it reverses the meaning, others say it weakens it or signifies an "unhealthy" way for the card's principle to manifest. To me, it makes the most sense to see an inverted card as an energy that is "stuck" or dampened and can't express itself fully. I'm curious what you think. Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Inverted tarot cards
replies.content:
Chiming in here from my own perspective:

In many of the oldest tarot decks, about half of the "number cards" in each suit look the just same upside-down as rightside-up. Because of this, I've never paid any attention to inversions when I read cards; I just lay down each card rightside-up to the querent's view, no matter how it comes out of the deck. And my readings do generally turn out to be useful for the querents.

However as Fritz Leiber once wrote, "Witchcraft and sorcery are games it takes two to play; the witch or sorcerer and his or her client." When I read cards, it is always for someone else, and I always do "cold-reading" on the querent as I am slowly laying out the cards. For me, the cards simply serve to nudge the cold-reading process in one direction or another. They don't have anything at all to say to me in and of themselves, and I've never been able to use them to divine for myself effectively.

As an aside, runes work very well for me, and the individual runes can and do actually speak to me in quite interesting and unexpected ways. I even get effective readings for myself from them now and then.

I suspect this affinity for runes, and indifference to tarot, has a lot to do with ancestry and upbringing. In ancestry I'm about 50% Scandinavian, and almost entirely Germanic, and the very first words I ever heard about any Gods at all were the old powerful tales in "Norse Gods and Giants" by the d'Aulaires.

replies.title: Re: Inverted tarot cards
replies.content:
Anon from above here - I'm actually German, and a Heathen too, but the runes as divination don't work for me at all. I usually can't figure out what they're trying to tell me because the principles behind them are too... large. It's like pouring water into a glass with too much water pressure and having it all gush out again. Any rune principle could mean 10 different things when I try to answer a specific question. Maybe that's a question of practice. But the tarot is more bite-sized (sure, it still has plenty of depth, but a single card has less "breadth" than a rune) and it's not too difficult for me to get useful answers so I try to get good at that for now.

I will say though that the two times I got a useful answer out of the runes, that answer stuck with me for a long time. (And once I got the distinct impression that they were being sarcastic because I was asking an obvious question I knew the answer to already. ;))

replies.title: Re: Inverted tarot cards
replies.content: How you read tarot cards is, and should be, unique to you. If you find in practice that that's what inverted cards mean for you, that's what they mean for you, and don't worry about how anybody else reads them.

comments.title: Rave book review - and a question
comments.content:
JMG – time for some fan mail and a question!

First the fan mail: I have just finished reading your book Paths of Wisdom and I must say that the work is a masterpiece. I liked Fortune’s The Mystical Qabalah well enough (it was my first foray into the Cabala and there was a lot for me to digest), but your book simply blew me away. Your writing about the Tiphareth and the Failed Mystic was a real “light bulb” moment for me. And by (so-called) coincidence, at the time that I read about Adam Cadmon, I was studying the Virata Purusha in the Hindu classic Srimad Bhagavat. There is so much material in the book that I will probably be working on it (at one level or another) for the next decade or two. Hats off to you, man!

Now for the question (which is not directly related to the Cabala). You have replied to many questions about magic and children quite a few times in the past – but this question is regarding young children using meditation or visualization exercises that in some ways resemble magic rituals. I know of a private school wherein the day starts with a guided meditation in which children (aged 4 to 13) are advised to visualize light (in the form of a flame) travelling throughout their body purifying their senses and mind, finally residing in the heart and expanding in ever larger circles to encompass the entire universe. In some ways it resembles the Sphere of Protection. I would like to ask your view about the influence of this type of meditation on young minds and whether or not it is a wise practice.

Ron M

replies.title: Re: Rave book review - and a question
replies.content:
Thank you! I wrote Paths of Wisdom because I wanted a book like it when I was doing my own intensive study of Golden Dawn Cabala, and nobody else seemed to be about to write one.

As for the guided meditation, good question. That's not something I would recommend on first principles, but if the school gets good results from the practice, that's a data point that should be taken seriously. Is it by any chance a Waldorf school?

replies.title: Re: Rave book review - and a question
replies.content: Thanks for the reply, JMG. No, the school is not a Waldorf school; it is one you’ve never heard of – the Sathya Sai School. The founder had advocated this form of meditation to adults, but I am not sure if he ever explicitly stated that children can do it. Both my children practiced this meditation daily for quite a few years and the one thing that I will note is that they are extremely mindful of never physically harming any “animal” (including insects) and are highly empathetic towards the suffering of others.

replies.title: Re: Prayer

replies.title: Re: Prayer
replies.content: Thanks a lot!

replies.title: Re: Prayer
replies.content: If you change the prayer you're going to get a different result, but since you want a different result, you need to change the prayer. Yes, it'll probably be less powerful at first, but the more you pray using your own prayers, the more powerful they will become. replies.title: Re: Prayer
replies.content:

replies.title: Re: Prayer
replies.content:
Hello Andrea,

If I may, I think you'd do a disservice to yourself by mutilating the Holy Rosary. Not that fire or brimstone is to fall on you, but you will be left with a pale facsimile of the original. There's an esoteric dimension of the Misteries in the Rosay (the 15 Traditional Ones, no comment on the Luminous innovation) that involves the Astrological influences.

However, you must be true to those feelings about Our Lady having power of her own, besides the person of her Son, Jesus. So, maybe you can use well established prayers that focus on her own powers and her roles towards us? I personally recomend "Hail, Holy Queen" for starters. I also do the Consacration to Mary (the version that says "my eyes, my ears, my tongue, my heart"), but I consider it serious stuff and not to be repeated verbatim, but consciously offered after a relationship has already been established.

Finally, I would not call the Hail Mary is "machista". IMHO it is simple recognition that Mary is not the Supreme Being, but subordinate to it. It is a cultural accident that the Supreme Being is perceived to be male (you may console yourself in the fact that, while the Bible do make reference to God's womb, it does not mention his penis at all). I'd recomend to meditate on her own words on the subject, as recorded in the Magnificat (which according to my mother serves as well as an invocation to be said under grave and immediate danger).

Best regards

CR Patino

comments.title: Druidry and Christianity
comments.content:
I've initially taken up "The Celtic Golden Dawn" for the very reason laid out in the introduction -- a wish to practice Golden Dawn-style magic without using Judaeo-Christian language. This is because I come from a staunchly Christian background and have gone away from it over the past several years, deliberately rejecting it as oppressive, among other things.

But over the last couple weeks of so, I've noticed a very unexpected occurrence. As I've been immersing myself in a daily pagan practice, my attitude has softened toward Christianity (at least certain expressions of it). And I've even been considering church attendance once again to engage myself in the liturgy, which I find to be quite beautiful (and magical!). And I'm interested in the potentiality of Christianity as a mystery religion with an initiatory path, although I think my wife would put up some resistance to any suggestion of returning to the church.

I realize, just by perusing the internet, there are plenty of folks who consider themselves both Druid and Christian, and there are certainly Christian Hermeticists. I suppose I may be interested in all of that.

Coincidentally, this year I also begun my journey as a freemason. I was initially turned off by all the Abrahamic language and myths, but I seem to have become accustomed to them; and as my recent change of attitude has occurred, I might even feel some comfort in them. And a recent reading of Albert Pike saying Druids had a concept of the trinity and other things make a Christopagan possibility seem real.

I don't want to be a flippant experimenter: I want to be a serious practitioner. And if this language is less bothersome to me now, I wonder why a Celtic Golden Dawn and not simply a Golden Dawn as it was originally presented in its Judaeo-Christian context. But I think I should just stick with what I've started, push on through, and see where it gets me. I am still very interested in pre-Christian, pagan myths and practices and want to be open to them.

So no matter where this leads me, is the Druidical Order of the Golden Dawn open to those who are members of one of the major world religions? And do you suspect that my new softening of heart is an indication of something serious or simply a nostalgic regressing back to what I knew last time I had a spiritual practice? I like simplicity but realize I have to face what to do with my Hermetical Masonic Anglo-Catholic Druidical interests. I wonder if I've previously set up a binary (reject Christianity as bad and see paganism as good) and am now considering a ternary of some kind.

replies.title: Re: Druidry and Christianity
replies.content:
Hi There,

I am a member of the Druidical Order of the Golden Dawn; I recently completed the second degree (that is, the Bardic grade). I was raised as a Roman Catholic, and rejected that faith for the reasons you rejected your own Christian upbringing. While I did initially start with traditional, Hermetic Golden Dawn work, I took up the Celtic Golden Dawn specifically to work with nature powers unrelated to the Judeo-Christian pantheon.

And yet, I have had the same experience as you-- over the course of years, my attitude toward sacramental Christianity (not freeform Protestantism, fwiw) softened, and in fact I started occasionally attending mass again (though not that often, as I find the contemporary guitar masses nearly intolerable). These days I am a registered parishioner at a church in town. My apartment is filled with more Catholic iconography than that of my Irish-American grandmother, and I pray the rosary almost every day. I also practice the magic of the Celtic Golden Dawn every day, and pray 4 times daily (that is, morning, noon, evening and night) to the elemental powers of the Druid pantheon. I'm aware of how upsetting this might be to a Catholic traditionalist, or for that matter to my grandmother, but I can't say I particularly care.

And not only is it not an issue in the Druidical Order of the Golden Dawn, it's not a unique experience. Another member of the order who was not raised Catholic has found himself powerfully drawn to the sacramentals and the iconography (and is blessed to live next to a shrine dedicated to Mary that is one of the most powerful spiritual places I have ever been, period). Others have encountered a being that JMG has named "the Green Christ."

What is going on? I have no idea. But I sometimes speculate along the following lines. I've read that Carl Jung was fond of a story about a sacred well. The well contained the Waters of Life, and they were freely available to all comers. But over time a small number of rich men took control of the well and built walls around it, charging ever higher fees for admission.

And the Water of Life left. It reappeared in a new place, and was again freely available... until another group of rich men got control of it and again put up walls and charged for admission. And the Water left again, and reappeared, and was again enclosed, and again found a new place, and so it goes, on and on and on, down to the present day.

I often think that the Powers that emerged into the world under the names of the Father, Son and Holy Ghost, the Blessed Virgin Mary, and the various angels and saints, have taken on new names and new forms, suitable for the spiritual needs of our times and different from their old names which have by and large had walls built around them by the powerful.

Maybe. Or maybe Hu and Jesus just get along with each other. Either way, a (nother) Hermetical Masonic Anglo-Catholic Druid would get along well in the DOGD.

-Steve

replies.title: Re: Druidry and Christianity
replies.content: Thank you very much for your thoughtful reply. It means a lot to me to hear that others are having similar experiences. And, of course, sacramental Christianity is the only form considered in my above statements. I believe there is a reason fundamentalist, evangelical Protestants cast away the sacramental aspects (candles, icons, statues, saints, etc.), and it is for the same reason I am attracted to those things: They are remnants of a pagan past.

replies.title: Re: Druidry and Christianity
replies.content:
I know it’s late, but I think the extra data point is important.

The exact same thing happened to me. I started out Protestant, converted to Witchcraft as a young teenager and never had any use for Christianity since. Then, because of some good advice from a certain Druid, I realized my practice might be unbalanced and I might benefit from a course of study that was deliberately balanced. I chose Celtic Golden Dawn, and immediately synchronicity drew me to a brand new order of Christian mystics.

Against the advice of said Druid, I began studying both paths simultaneously because synchronicity seemed to direct me that way (and for me synchronicity trumps all other sources of good advice lol) and within 6 months I find myself trying to merge Pagan and Catholic beliefs. Let me tell you, I did NOT see that coming. I think I have settled on a form of Paganism that embraces Christianity (including the saints and angels) as another available pantheon of Gods, alongside the Pagan deities I also love dearly.

I wonder if it is merely coincidence, or if the fusion of beliefs is somehow related to the Pope speaking out about climate change, and his meeting with the Orthodox Patriarch where they both condemned climate-changing activities..... Could it be a sign that spiritual forces are drawing both paths closer together?

Or my other theory is if you leave the religion of your birth, eventually in your later, mature practice, you might have to heal the wound that drove you away from your first religion in order to grow beyond it. All religions should draw us closer to the Gods, and if the religion of your birth does not work for you, there is a real, important reason, important enough to need to be dealt with later. This is especially true if a different religion did draw you later. It probably doesn’t apply to those people who have no use for religions or Gods. Just my two cents.

Sincerely
Jessi Thompson
anotheramethyst

replies.title: Re: Druidry and Christianity
replies.content: There have been plenty of Christian Druids since the beginnings of the Druid Revival in the early 18th century, and most of them do exactly as you're doing -- revering the deities of Celtic Pagan faith and those of Christianity. Thus you're very nearly in the mainstream of the tradition. As for the DOGD, the order does not presume to interfere in the spiritual lives of its members; it does require that members practice the rituals as given in the textbook The Celtic Golden Dawn without alteration, but who else you pray to is your business, not the business of the order.

It appears that nearly all occult systems discuss and attempt to manipulate some kind of "life force". OK that seems likely and I can accept that as a working hypothesis.

Now my question is: Is this a limited resource? I am always leery of things that are touted as "infinite or unlimited".

replies.title: Re: May the Force be..

replies.title: Re: May the Force be..
replies.content: A fine question! The life force is a renewable resource; it has certain limits, and you can overuse it to the extent that you can harm yourself, but that takes hard work. You generate more of it by being alive, and so does every other living thing. What's more, you already use and direct the life force, and you always have; so does every other living thing. Learning magic just teaches you to do intelligently what you've been doing in an unthinking manner all along.

comments.title: Zen + Magic(k)
comments.content:
I came recently into magic (with help from your books, thanks!) from a couple of years of Zen practice. My thought is that Zen sitting has been an excellent "intro course" and I'd love to read your response to a relation I feel between zen sitting and Binah.

Especially when there is a "problem" in your life, you would sit with it and form of yourself a fireplace, with the problem serving as the wood to heat up the structure. What comes next, after time, is a deep feeling of compassion for the personality with the problem, but a firm commitment to hold, sit with, and accept reality as it is. I think I really grok Binah now, as a place of recognition that the constraint of Form is for the best. The wondrous thing is that it remains a "loving Mother," fully understanding the consequences of that constraint, and holding the constraint firmly yet with the greatest empathy.

I guess it still makes sense that Enlightenment in the Zen sense is a breaking through to Kether, but when I think about how Zen actually functions in the lives of many practitioners, I have this sense that Binah is the most important "feeling-tone."

Thanks for any thoughts! I'm also *really* enjoying the Cosmic Doctrine Book Club, just a shout out for that, too :).

replies.title: Re: Zen + Magic(k)
replies.content: That doesn't surprise me at all. Buddhism has always seemed to me to have a strong Binah flavor to it. Most religions have a specific sphere of the Tree at their core; Christianity, for example, is a Tiphareth faith -- thus it has the positive qualities of Tiphareth when it's in tune with itself, and it quickly turns to open war between out-of-control solar egos when it's not in tune with itself.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
First of all, don't participate in the workings of the Magic Resistance, or you risk getting caught up in the blowback. You can't change something like that from within; what'll happen is that you'll be swept along with the momentum of the egregor, and flying face first with the rest of them into all those disasters.

Second, remember that effective magic focuses on strengthening and empowering the things you want, not tearing down the things you don't want. If you dislike Trump, hey, that's understandable, and it's also your right as a citizen to disagree with him. If you want to use magic in that cause, focus on the things you want to strengthen. If you want to inspire this nation to uphold the values of liberty and tolerance, say, that's a workable magical intention; the Statue of Liberty is a very effective image for work along those lines, though it's not the only one. Choose your positive and constructive goal, design a working tautly focused on that alone, and keep your mouth shut about it, and you can have a significant impact.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I'm chiming in late here. My own sense, after looking into the literature for a quarter-century, is that the "energy" or "force" that makes magic possible is not a constant thing like, say, gravity, but more resembles a living being. That is, it possesses something akin to volition or choice.

It can--and sometimes does--refuse to coöperate in a magical working, for reasons need not have anything to do with the magician or the magician's preparation and training, or the design of the working, or even its purpose.

Whenever it chooses not to coöperate, then nothing happens. When it chooses to coöperate, sometimes even a clumsy amateur magician can get spectacular results.

If this is so, then controlled experiments, and even the scientific method generally, would seem to be very hard to bring to bear on the problem.

For what it might be worth, the usual Greek word for this "power" or "force" behind magic appears to be dynamis, which is a noun from the Greek verb dynamai, meaning something like "to be capable" (of doing something), "to have the power" (to do something).

comments.content: I have a question about divination. I find I'm quite good at it, for specific questions (ex: what will happen if I take a job, ask so and so out, etc). I can't for the life of me get a good answer to "what do I most need to know about the day ahead?". Do you have advice for getting that skill, aside from practicing more?

replies.title: clear answers to a class of questions
replies.content:
Possibly it would help to vary your divination method.

Tarot seems to me to be well suited to answer "what do I most need to know about the day ahead?", but if it isn't working for you, you might need either to narrow down the symbol set for more clear cut answers or, conversely, widen it to the point where you are basically scrying for messages from your unconscious mind.

Staying in the realm of cards, you could try the question with one of the many oracle decks--they seem to be geared to giving spiritual advice and suggestions about energies. Or go the opposite direction and buy a Lenormand deck with unfussy art and a good guidebook--the Lenormand cards seem to be optimized for practical answers like "avoid getting into a traffic accident" or "good day to buy flowers".

Or change tools completely. If you have been reading cards, you could for example get a bunch of pebbles or glass flower-arrangement stones, paint them with symbols of the planets, elements and signs of the Zodiac, and pull three stones every morning.

I use the I Ching when I want to know how to deal with people in organizations in ways that will further my goals. If most of what concerns you in the average day is dealing with your business, large bureaucratic organizations, a bunch of clients, obtaining backers and finance, etc. , the I Ching is happy to advise you on whom to approach and how hard to push. If your job is going out in the wild looking for illegal grow sites or trees about to fall on power lines, a pendulum over a map would be more useful.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That question may not be well suited to your personal gifts. Most people get good results from it, but you may not be "most people;" in that case, come up with specific questions to ask every day, and focus on that instead.

comments.title: Neighbor troubles
comments.content:
Hello,

Seeking advice on how to help my older aunt whose neighbor's middle-aged son and daughter are cutting my aunt's hedges along the property line. The hedges have been there for several years, are well kept, and don't overhang into the neighbor's property. The previous neighbor was extremely finicky about his yard and never had issues with these hedges.

When the new neighbor moved in, she and my aunt exchanged pleasantries and even phone numbers. A few months later, my aunt first noticed the hedges looking thinner. She didn't tell the neighbor what she noticed but did ask if the hedges bothered her. She told my aunt they did not.

The way in which the neighbor's adult kids are cutting the hedges leads me to believe they are  suspicious of my aunt. Namely, they have thinned out several mid-sections so they can more easily see my aunt's driveway and door from their windows. As to why they may harbor suspicions, I can only guess. My aunt is single, older, quiet, and respectful. I can assure you she's done nothing to antagonize them. She's lived there for years and never had problems. Now this is a pretty heterogeneous town, and my aunt is ethnic and an immigrant. Perhaps that's a source of discomfort for them--I don't know.

After she had a security camera installed, the cutting stopped for a while. (Interestingly, there's footage of what looks to be the neighbor's daughter flipping off the camera.) But now, the hedges that are out of view of the camera are getting thinner.

replies.title: unbalanced lunar influences
replies.content:
For about the first fifteen years that I practiced witchcraft, I worked almost exclusively with lunar and telluric powers, because those were the ones that attracted me. I made rapid progress for a while, but eventually began to feel stale. I came to the conclusion that I was experiencing the psychic equivalent of a nutritional deficiency.

I knew people who were active in Crowley's OTO, which has a predominantly solar current. I joined the OTO. A lot of what they did and taught was not congenial to me, but I found ways into it, and it had the effect I desired, changing the balance of my outlook and introducing me to the rudiments of ceremonial magic.

In the more popular forms of Wicca, solar influence is to be found mainly in the cycle of seasonal holidays, aka the Wheel of the Year. Some traditions use a mythical story about the life of the Horned God, associating him with the sun's journey through the seasons. Other varieties of Wicca, primarily the older traditions, include more teaching and practices derived from ceremonial magic. These bring solar energy and astrological influences into people's regular personal practice.

People who have the opportunity to choose among different varieties of Wicca often gravitate to one that directly addresses what they feel is lacking in their lives or psyches. Often the desire is for a connection with female power which in Wicca is strongly associated with the moon, or the life force of the earth, or to access the unconscious, or resolve the duality of light and darkness. Once they have remedied whatever they were missing, they may seek out other energies.

comments.content: Are retrograde planets necessarily malefic?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Not at all. Retrogradation has been turned into a bogeyman by pop astrologers, and it doesn't deserve that status.

In a natal chart, retrograde planets have various effects, but generally they indicate that whatever aspect of life they govern (judging this from the nature of the planet, and also from the house cusps they rule) will ripen only late in life. That can be a positive thing depending on how the retrograde planet relates to other planets! (For example, I have Mercury retrograde applying to a trine with Saturn; that's made my writing career develop slowly, but it also tells me that my career will reach its zenith very late in life, and (together with certain other indications) that I'll have plenty of money in old age.

In transits -- i.e., what the planets are doing right now -- a retrograde is favorable for anything that begins with the prefix "re-". Consistently, for example, I do my best revising and rewriting when Mercury is retrograde.

comments.title: Hellenistic Heaven?
comments.content:
Hello JMG.

I recall you mentioning on at least several occasions that the various gods potentially will offer their particular heaven to a faithful adherent when said adherent’s time on earth is done, and that it is worthwhile, while you are alive, to let your god/goddess know you would like to obtain admission to his/her heaven. This sets up one pole of my question.

For the other pole: I am theistic, but I have nonetheless long felt that the throne of my home religion was vacant, and I have felt quite alone with respect to the spirit world (and my incarnate world is not much better). Then I read your advice, about a year ago, to direct one’s prayers to the spirit world requesting to be heard by any unknown, beneficent deity, who might be willing to work with me, and to kindly reveal him/herself as my patron deity. I thought that was good advice: I could honestly manifest some spiritual yearning in a regular spiritual practice that did not feel delusional or hopeless. After about 6 months of such nightly prayers, I experienced a series of startling coincidences that convinced me that I had been heard by the Hellenistic god Hermes, and that he condescended to be my patron god. Needless to say, I accepted him with relief and gratitude. I don’t as yet feel too much connection with the other gods, though I have recently learned to respect and value Ares (he gets tons of bad press, starting with Homer), as I feel he has somehow communicated/inspired the correct use of personal boundaries, which has proved unexpectedly helpful for my personal confidence… The minor (for lack of a better word) goddess Iaso, daughter of Asclepius, goddess of recovery, also prompted insights I wish I had decades ago.

Now my question: It is my understanding that the ancient Hellenistic gods did not offer heaven to their adherents. I recall from a Dialog of Plato (The Apology IIRC), that one of the reasons death seemed so fearsome is that you loose access to pretty much all of the gods of the Pantheon. Basically, you get to meet Hermes for a bit when he escorts you down to the underworld, and then you hang out with Hades forevermore, with nothing thereafter to write home about. Socrates (and presumably Plato) had some faith that they would encounter other gods who were at least as beneficent and wonderful as the gods of the Pantheon, but I don’t know that their faith was based on anything than their personal hopes. I know of no corroborating mythology (hmm...do I need to do my homework?). All told, as grateful as I am to Hermes for offering me spiritual companionship and guidance, and as precious as I find the gods and their wisdom, the absence of a Hellenistic heaven seems rather stark to say the least. How can I ask for a place in heaven if the god and his pantheon don’t have one to offer? Yet the thought of turning away from a god who answered my prayers and seems to care about me, feels like betrayal. What would you do?

replies.title: Re: Hellenistic Heaven?
replies.content:
You definitely have to do your homework! The Orphic texts make it clear that there's an alternative to hanging out as a shade in the Underworld, a la the terrifying scene in Homer; the rich traditions of Greek Pagan mysticism built on that, and in the writings of great Pagan Neoplatonists such as Iamblichus and Proclus, the details are set out clearly.

What happens when you die, if you worship a particular god or goddess and practice the appropriate rites, is that you become one of the companions of the deity. In the astrological symbolism that was so much in use among the Neoplatonists, the gods were equated with the planets, and portrayed symbolically as journeying through the skies in a celebratory procession, surrounded by their servant daimones and the once-human companion souls who had been lifted up to that condition by the practice of virtue and devotion.

replies.title: Re: Hellenistic Heaven?

replies.title: Re: Alternatives to Mage-hood
replies.content:
Okay, first of all, yes, you should probably add in the Middle Pillar. Do it right after the LBRP, before your meditation, and keep doing it daily for another three or four months. Then, if you want to go on, take Regardie's instructions in Ceremonial Magic and follow them exactly. It'll take you something like a year to finish, but by the time you get there, you'll have initiated yourself into Golden Dawn magic in a very solid and effective way, and you can then go on to other magical practices.

Each one is a complete path, as demanding and as full of potential as the page of the mage. Most people are drawn primarily to one and secondarily to another; a good magical training in the Hermetic tradition involves some exposure to all of them.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Should a beginner/neophyte of the golden dawn practice the greater ritual of the pentagram as well as the lesser?
Also can you do the greater ritual daily instead of the lesser?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Nope. If you're a beginner, you should stick to the Lesser Ritual of the Pentagram, full stop, end of sentence. Do the invoking version every morning and the banishing version every evening. Do this daily for at least two months before you add any other magical practices, and when you do add something, what you add should be the Middle Pillar exercise.

Once you start daily Middle Pillar exercises, you can drop the invoking ritual -- the Middle Pillar is itself an invocation -- and simply do the Lesser Banishing Ritual of the Pentagram followed by the Middle Pillar once every day. Do this for the rest of your first year of practice. Magic is like athletics -- it won't do you a bit of good to try to compete out there in the field until you've spent a good long time doing the exercises that build the strength and skills you need.

Remember, you're going to need to put a lot of time into study; you need to learn the symbolism of the Tree of Life inside and out, practice Tarot divination and geomancy, and study all the other things a Golden Dawn practitioner needs to know. That's what you should be doing in your first year of training. Then, having built a solid foundation through study and practice of the basics, you can move on to more advanced practices such as the elemental and supreme rituals of the pentagram.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) No, you just won't have gotten much useful out of it. Buckle down to the work of the LRP and build those skills!

2) Depends on which Golden Dawn organization or teacher you ask. In the original Golden Dawn, remember, you were eligible to advance to the Zelator grade after just one month as a Neophyte. I recommend that beginners start out by learning and practicing the LRP, and then spend the next couple of months learning the Hebrew alphabet and the names and basic symbolism of the Sephiroth of the Tree of Life, and doing some basic reading -- for example, Dion Fortune's book The Mystical Qabalah and W.E. Butler's The Magician: His Training and Work. After that you can add the Middle Pillar to your practice regimen, take up geomancy, and go deeper into Cabalistic studies.
